(July 11, 1772 – August 1, 1838) was a [United States Navy](/wiki/United_States_Navy "United States Navy") officer who served during the Navy's formative years from the 1790s through to the late 1830s. He served under six [presidents](/wiki/Presidents_of_the_United_States "Presidents of the United States") and for nearly four decades. His service took him through numerous military engagements of the [Quasi-War](/wiki/Quasi-War "Quasi-War"), both [Barbary Wars](/wiki/Barbary_Wars "Barbary Wars"), and the [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812").

[Commodore](/wiki/Commodore_(United_States) "Commodore (United States)") **John Rodgers** (July 11, 1772 – August 1, 1838) was a [United States Navy](/wiki/United_States_Navy "United States Navy") officer who served during the Navy's formative years from the 1790s through to the late 1830s. He served under six [presidents](/wiki/Presidents_of_the_United_States "Presidents of the United States") and for nearly four decades. His service took him through numerous military engagements of the [Quasi-War](/wiki/Quasi-War "Quasi-War"), both [Barbary Wars](/wiki/Barbary_Wars "Barbary Wars"), and the [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812").

As a senior officer in the young American navy, Rodgers played a major role in the development of the standards, customs and traditions that emerged during this time. Rodgers was, among other things, noted for commanding the largest American [squadron](/wiki/Squadron_(naval) "Squadron (naval)") in his day to sail the [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea").[[1]](#cite_note-paullin1-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) After serving with distinction as a lieutenant, he was soon promoted directly to the rank of [captain](/wiki/Captain_(United_States) "Captain (United States)") (the rank of [Master Commandant](/wiki/Master_Commandant "Master Commandant") did not exist at that time).[[3]](#cite_note-3) During his naval career he commanded a number of warships, including [USS *John Adams*](/wiki/USS_John_Adams_(1799) "USS John Adams (1799)"), the [flagship](/wiki/Flagship "Flagship") of the fleet that defeated the [Barbary states](/wiki/Barbary_states "Barbary states") of North Africa.

During the War of 1812, Rodgers fired the first shot of the war aboard his next flagship, [USS *President*](/wiki/USS_President_(1800) "USS President (1800)"), and also played a leading role in the reoccupation of [Washington D.C.](/wiki/Washington_D.C. "Washington D.C.") after it was [occupied by British forces](/wiki/Burning_of_Washington "Burning of Washington"). His own hometown of [Havre de Grace, Maryland](/wiki/Havre_de_Grace,_Maryland "Havre de Grace, Maryland") was raided and burnt during the war. Later in his career he headed the [Board of Navy Commissioners](/wiki/Board_of_Navy_Commissioners "Board of Navy Commissioners"), and he served briefly as [Secretary of the Navy](/wiki/Secretary_of_the_Navy_(United_States) "Secretary of the Navy (United States)").[[4]](#cite_note-Paullin,_1910_p.325-4) Following in his footsteps, Rodgers' son, and several grandsons and great-grandsons, also became commodores and admirals in the United States Navy.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Rodgers_(naval_officer,_born_1772)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Rodgers's parents were part of a large wave of Scottish immigrants to the [Thirteen Colonies](/wiki/Thirteen_Colonies "Thirteen Colonies") in the years prior to the [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution"). His father, John Rodgers, was born in [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland") in 1726. He emigrated to America and in 1760 married Elizabeth Reynolds (born 1742) from [Delaware](/wiki/Delaware "Delaware"), who was also of Scots ancestry. They had eight children, four sons and four daughters; the younger John Rodgers was named for his father.[[Note 1]](#cite_note-7) Like many other Scots immigrants, his father became a proponent of the patriot cause and served as a colonel in the militia.

Rodgers was born in 1772 on a farm in a village near the "Susquehanna Ferry", on the north shore of the [Susquehanna River](/wiki/Susquehanna_River "Susquehanna River") (near today's [Perryville](/wiki/Perryville,_Maryland "Perryville, Maryland")) in [Cecil County](/wiki/Cecil_County,_Maryland "Cecil County, Maryland"). This was near its mouth at the Chesapeake Bay. He was raised here for the first thirteen years of his life. While Rodgers was still a youth, the village on the south shore (in [Harford County](/wiki/Harford_County,_Maryland "Harford County, Maryland")) was named *"[Havre de Grace](/wiki/Havre_de_Grace,_Maryland "Havre de Grace, Maryland")"* by [Marquis de Lafayette](/wiki/Gilbert_du_Motier,_marquis_de_Lafayette "Gilbert du Motier, marquis de Lafayette") after a famous French port of the same name. The young Rodgers was known to fish in the Susquehanna and Chesapeake Bay near his home. He attended local school, and read books about the seafaring life. He had often seen schooner-rigged ships in Havre de Grace but longed to see the large square-rigged vessels he had always read about.

Realizing Rodgers was determined to go to sea, his father helped arrange his apprenticeship with Captain Benjamin Folger, a master ship builder of Baltimore, and Revolutionary War veteran. He had served aboard merchant ships and as commander of *Felicity*, used in the capture of a [privateer](/wiki/Privateer "Privateer"). By this time, Folger was captain and owner of *Maryland*. Rodgers served a five-year apprenticeship on this ship.[[7]](#cite_note-8)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/Mrs._John_Rodgers_%28Minerva_Denison%29.jpg/250px-Mrs._John_Rodgers_%28Minerva_Denison%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mrs._John_Rodgers_(Minerva_Denison).jpg)

Minerva Denison Rodgers, portrait by [John Wesley Jarvis](/wiki/John_Wesley_Jarvis "John Wesley Jarvis"), c. 1806

In 1806 Rodgers married Minerva Denison;[[8]](#cite_note-Paullin398-9) they had three sons, Robert, Frederick and John, and two daughters together.[[Note 2]](#cite_note-10) Their son [John Rodgers Jr.](/wiki/John_Rodgers_(American_Civil_War_naval_officer) "John Rodgers (American Civil War naval officer)") was born in Maryland in 1812. He entered the [U.S. Navy](/wiki/United_States_Navy "United States Navy") as a [midshipman](/wiki/Midshipman "Midshipman"), serving aboard the Baltimore-built [USS *Constellation*](/wiki/USS_Constellation_(1797) "USS Constellation (1797)") and [USS *Concord*](/wiki/USS_Concord_(1828) "USS Concord (1828)") in the [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea"). Later he was commissioned as a [rear admiral](/wiki/Rear_admiral_(United_States) "Rear admiral (United States)") during the [Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"). Rodgers also had several grandsons and great-grandsons who became officers in the U.S. Navy.[[9]](#cite_note-11)

### First command

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Rodgers_(naval_officer,_born_1772)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: First command")]

When Rodgers was seventeen, Captain Folger promoted him to [first mate](/wiki/First_mate "First mate") of the merchant ship *Harmony*. By the time Rodgers completed his five years of apprenticeship in 1793, Folger highly recommended him for command of *Jane*, a merchant ship regularly used in the European trade and owned by Baltimore merchants Samuel and John Smith.[[10]](#cite_note-Paullin20-21-12) Rodgers served as the captain of this ship between four and five years, sailing out of Baltimore for various ports in Europe. His first voyage took him to the Spanish port of [Cádiz](/wiki/C%C3%A1diz "Cádiz") in the early months of 1793, returning home with a load of salt. Rodgers' next voyage sent him to [Hamburg](/wiki/Hamburg "Hamburg"), Germany, but due to severe conditions on the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea"), he was forced to put up in England for the winter and did not reach his destination until spring of the next year. In September 1795 he departed for Baltimore from [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool "Liverpool"), arriving after a passage of three months. Logbooks of the *Jane* during Rodgers's command cover the period of July–August 1796 in detail, a time when [France](/wiki/French_First_Republic "French First Republic") and [Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain") were still [at war](/wiki/War_of_the_First_Coalition "War of the First Coalition").[[Note 3]](#cite_note-14) During this period, Rodgers mastered the art of ship's command.[[11]](#cite_note-Paullin25-26-13)

Rodgers and his crew were tested under severe conditions on the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea"). Winds had carried the ship off course, provisions were almost exhausted, and three of his crewmen had frozen to death in one night. Most of the rest despaired of survival. When Rodgers ordered some of the crew to go aloft to secure ice-encrusted rigging, they refused. Outraged, Rodgers stripped off his jacket and shirt and, before going aloft, told the crew to watch what a man could do. While he climbed the frozen rigging bare-chested, the crew immediately rose to his aid. They soon secured the faltering rigging. Given the grim conditions, Rodgers put the matter behind him and days later, they safely reached port.[[12]](#cite_note-15)

## Naval career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Rodgers_(naval_officer,_born_1772)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Naval career")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/John_Rodgers_%28naval_officer%2C_War_of_1812%29.jpg/250px-John_Rodgers_%28naval_officer%2C_War_of_1812%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_Rodgers_(naval_officer,_War_of_1812).jpg)

Rodgers portrait in 1798

Rodgers' service in the United States Navy extended through the [Quasi War](/wiki/Quasi_War "Quasi War") with France, the [First Barbary War](/wiki/First_Barbary_War "First Barbary War") and the [Second Barbary War](/wiki/Second_Barbary_War "Second Barbary War") in [North Africa](/wiki/North_Africa "North Africa") and through the [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812"). In 1815 he was appointed to the Board of Naval Commissioners, serving through the Second Barbary War until he retired in 1837.

### Quasi War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Rodgers_(naval_officer,_born_1772)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Quasi War")]

On March 8, 1798, President [John Adams](/wiki/John_Adams "John Adams") appointed junior officers for the first three ships constructed for the young American Navy;[[Note 4]](#cite_note-17) Rodgers was appointed second lieutenant of the [frigate](/wiki/Frigate "Frigate") USS *Constellation*, under the command of [Thomas Truxtun](/wiki/Thomas_Truxtun "Thomas Truxtun"). All of these officers were expeditiously confirmed by the Senate the next day. Rodgers participated in the capture of the [French Navy](/wiki/French_Navy "French Navy") frigate [*L'Insurgente*](/wiki/USS_Insurgent "USS Insurgent")[[Note 5]](#cite_note-19) during *Constellation*'s [engagement](/wiki/Action_of_9_February_1799 "Action of 9 February 1799"), and he immediately was made prize master of the surrendered French vessel. Rodgers, along with Midshipman Porter and eleven seamen, boarded the badly damaged *L'Insurgente* with the challenge of sailing her to a friendly port while also guarding more than 160 prisoners.[[15]](#cite_note-20) That evening, gale-force winds separated the two ships, leaving Rodgers, Porter, and the few American seamen aboard the now-renamed *Insurgent* to save the ship and to control the prisoners without support from the crew of *Constellation* nearby. To make matters worse, just before surrendering their ship, the crew of *L'Insurgente* had thrown overboard the gratings to the hold along with handcuffs and other items used to secure prisoners. Greatly outnumbered, Rodgers had seized all weapons and ordered their prisoners to the [lower hold](/wiki/Hold_(compartment) "Hold (compartment)"),[[16]](#cite_note-21) giving orders to open fire with [blunderbusses](/wiki/Blunderbuss "Blunderbuss") should they try to breach the passageway from their hold. After guarding the prisoners and navigating the captured vessel for two days and three nights through stormy winter weather, Rodgers arrived at [Bassettere](/w/index.php?title=Bassettere&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bassettere (page does not exist)"), the capital of the [British colony](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") of [Saint Kitts](/wiki/Saint_Kitts "Saint Kitts"), on February 13, 1799. Great Britain and France were [still at war](/wiki/War_of_the_Second_Coalition "War of the Second Coalition"), so the inhabitants of Bassettere were pleased to see a captured French vessel arriving in American hands. For the Americans' effort, a British commander in St. Kitts sent Truxtun a letter of congratulations and offered him every service within his command.[[17]](#cite_note-22) The two ships were then refitted and supplied while *Insurgent* received a new crew. On March 5, 1799, Rodgers was promoted to captain and received written orders to take command of the captured ship.[[14]](#cite_note-Paullin49-18)

In June 1799, Rodgers relinquished command of *Insurgent*, then at [Norfolk, Virginia](/wiki/Norfolk,_Virginia "Norfolk, Virginia"), receiving a letter from [Secretary of the Navy](/wiki/Secretary_of_the_Navy_(United_States) "Secretary of the Navy (United States)") [Benjamin Stoddert](/wiki/Benjamin_Stoddert "Benjamin Stoddert") ordering him to Baltimore to supervise the outfitting of [USS *Maryland*](/wiki/USS_Maryland_(1799) "USS Maryland (1799)"), a [sloop-of-war](/wiki/Sloop-of-war "Sloop-of-war")[[Note 6]](#cite_note-23) bearing 20 guns, and then to take command of that ship.[[18]](#cite_note-24) Three months later *Maryland* was commissioned under Rodgers' command. In March 1801, he delivered to France the ratified [Convention of 1800 (Treaty of Mortefontaine)](/wiki/Convention_of_1800_(Treaty_of_Mortefontaine) "Convention of 1800 (Treaty of Mortefontaine)"), which ended the [Quasi-War](/wiki/Quasi-War "Quasi-War").[[19]](#cite_note-25)

### First Barbary War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Rodgers_(naval_officer,_born_1772)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: First Barbary War")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/EnterpriseTripoli.jpg/250px-EnterpriseTripoli.jpg)](/wiki/File:EnterpriseTripoli.jpg)

USS *Enterprise* engaging *Tripoli*

Placed in command of [USS *John Adams*](/wiki/USS_John_Adams_(1799) "USS John Adams (1799)") on May 3 of the following year, Rodgers was ordered to sail for [Tripoli](/wiki/Tripoli,_Libya "Tripoli, Libya") to patrol its surrounding waters for three weeks, joining [USS *Constitution*](/wiki/USS_Constitution "USS Constitution") and [USS *President*](/wiki/USS_President_(1800) "USS President (1800)"), along with a number of other vessels. Upon his arrival he immediately approached the harbor fortifications of Tripoli and engaged the [gunboats](/wiki/Gunboat "Gunboat") and [batteries](/wiki/Coastal_artillery "Coastal artillery") defending the city. During this time he also pursued and boarded several neutral ships that were attempting to bring grain and other supplies to Tripoli, the inhabitants of which were facing starvation and other difficulties because of the blockade.[[20]](#cite_note-26) After twelve days *John Adams* encountered the Tripolian vessel Meshboha, bearing 20 guns, which Rodgers engaged and captured. The Tripolian vessel previously had been blockaded at [Gibraltar](/wiki/Gibraltar "Gibraltar") and was carrying a load of military supplies to Tripoli.[[21]](#cite_note-27) His brilliant record fighting the corsairs won Rodgers appointment as [commodore](/wiki/Commodore_(United_States) "Commodore (United States)") of the [Mediterranean Squadron](/wiki/Mediterranean_Squadron_(United_States) "Mediterranean Squadron (United States)") in May 1805. Since Commodore [James Barron's](/wiki/James_Barron "James Barron") health at this time had deteriorated, it was practically impossible for Barron to maintain command of the [squadron](/wiki/Squadron_(naval) "Squadron (naval)"). Receiving a letter dispatched to him by [USS *Essex*](/wiki/USS_Essex_(1799) "USS Essex (1799)") on May 22, Rodgers assumed command of the squadron consisting of the ships *Constitution*, *President*, *Constellation*, [*Enterprise*](/wiki/USS_Enterprise_(1799) "USS Enterprise (1799)"), *Essex*, [*Siren*](/wiki/USS_Syren_(1803) "USS Syren (1803)"), [*Argus*](/wiki/USS_Argus_(1803) "USS Argus (1803)"), [*Hornet*](/wiki/USS_Hornet_(1805_sloop) "USS Hornet (1805 sloop)"), [*Vixen*](/wiki/USS_Vixen_(1803) "USS Vixen (1803)"), [*Nautilus*](/wiki/USS_Nautilus_(1799) "USS Nautilus (1799)"), and [*Franklin*](/wiki/USS_Franklin_(1795) "USS Franklin (1795)"), together with a number of gunboats (including *[No. 5](/wiki/HMS_Ambush_(1814) "HMS Ambush (1814)")*) and [bomb vessels](/wiki/Bomb_vessel "Bomb vessel"). Rodgers was thus in command of the largest American squadron to assemble in the Mediterranean until the twentieth century. The blockading force was so overwhelming that, after much deliberation and appeals from the [Dey](/wiki/Dey "Dey"), a peace treaty with Tripoli was negotiated by the end of July.[[22]](#cite_note-28)

When news of the treaty reached [Washington D.C.](/wiki/Washington_D.C. "Washington D.C.") in the fall of 1805, President [Thomas Jefferson](/wiki/Thomas_Jefferson "Thomas Jefferson") ordered all of the ships home with the exception of a frigate and two smaller supporting vessels. Before returning home, Rodgers sailed to [Malta](/wiki/Malta "Malta") and [Syracuse](/wiki/Syracuse_(Sicily) "Syracuse (Sicily)") to close down military hospitals and settle accounts. He stopped to pay a visit to the Dey of [Algiers](/wiki/Algiers "Algiers"), who had learned of the US treaty with Tripoli, and extended every courtesy to Rodgers, allowing him to be armed in his presence. In a letter to the Secretary of the Navy, Benjamin Stoddert, Rodgers later wrote "I am the first Christian that has ever been permitted to visit the Dey of Algiers with sidearms...."[[23]](#cite_note-29)

### Other service

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Rodgers_(naval_officer,_born_1772)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Other service")]

A year later, Rodgers returned to the United States to take command of the [New York Flotilla](/w/index.php?title=New_York_Flotilla&action=edit&redlink=1 "New York Flotilla (page does not exist)"). After the [Embargo Act of 1807](/wiki/Embargo_Act_of_1807 "Embargo Act of 1807") against trade with the British Empire was passed by Congress at the close of 1807, Rodgers commanded operations along the Atlantic coast enforcing its provisions.

### Torpedo test

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Rodgers_(naval_officer,_born_1772)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Torpedo test")]

In 1810 Secretary of the Navy [Paul Hamilton](/wiki/Paul_Hamilton_(politician) "Paul Hamilton (politician)") instructed Commodore Rodgers to oversee a series of tests or trials of inventor [Robert Fulton](/wiki/Robert_Fulton "Robert Fulton")'s new naval [torpedo](/wiki/Torpedo "Torpedo"). Fulton had recently published *Torpedo War, and Submarine Explosions*, in which he argued that his newly developed torpedo was practical, and cost efficient, and would soon make most naval vessels obsolete. Presidents Thomas Jefferson and [James Madison](/wiki/James_Madison "James Madison") were intrigued by Fulton's idea and the [US Congress](/wiki/US_Congress "US Congress") authorized $5,000 to test the new weapon. Rodgers thought Fulton's torpedo was a folly. Secretary Hamilton directed Rodgers to prepare "a plan of opposition." In reply, Rodgers promised Hamilton he "would not only prevent the application of any torpedoes which he has yet invented, but any which he will ever be able to invent…"

On September 24, 1810, several thousand residents of New York City gathered on the banks of [Corlear's Hook](/wiki/Corlear%27s_Hook "Corlear's Hook") overlooking the [East River](/wiki/East_River "East River") to watch the widely publicized demonstration of a mock torpedo attack against the brig USS *Argus*, commanded by Lieutenant [James Lawrence](/wiki/James_Lawrence "James Lawrence"). Fulton, the successful inventor of the first commercially viable [steamboat](/wiki/Steamboat "Steamboat"), *[Clermont](/wiki/North_River_Steamboat "North River Steamboat")*, and the first practical submarine, [*Nautilus*](/wiki/Nautilus_(1800_submarine) "Nautilus (1800 submarine)"), was confident that his torpedo could sink a large naval vessel. Rodgers and Lt. Lawrence quickly demonstrated that Fulton's torpedo was unable to penetrate the ship's defense. Fulton in a final report to Hamilton reluctantly conceded he could not penetrate Rodgers' defense. While Rodgers was vindicated, the two men held no rancor toward each other. During the [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812") Fulton wrote Rodgers on September 14, 1814, to offer his service in defense of the port of Baltimore; before the letter arrived, the battle was over.[[24]](#cite_note-30)

At the outbreak of the War of 1812, the American navy was not prepared to deal with Britain's large and formidable navy, which consisted of hundreds of ships and seasoned commanders and crews, many of whom were already battle hardened by the [French Revolutionary and Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/French_Revolutionary_and_Napoleonic_Wars "French Revolutionary and Napoleonic Wars").[[25]](#cite_note-Roosevelt_p.72-31) In 1811, Rodgers was commodore of USS *President* off [Annapolis](/wiki/Annapolis "Annapolis") when he heard that an American sailor had been [impressed](/wiki/Impressment "Impressment") by [HMS *Guerriere*](/wiki/HMS_Guerriere_(1806) "HMS Guerriere (1806)") off [Sandy Hook](/wiki/Sandy_Hook_(New_Jersey) "Sandy Hook (New Jersey)"), [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey"). Rodgers was ordered to sea to "protect American commerce", but he may have had verbal instructions to retaliate for the [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy")'s impressment of alleged British deserters from American vessels, which was causing much ill-feeling.[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChisholm1911-32)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/The_Little_Belt.jpg/250px-The_Little_Belt.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Little_Belt.jpg)

USS *President* engaging HMS *Little Belt*

Early in 1811, Secretary of the Navy Hamilton had ordered USS *President* and USS *Argus* on patrol duty along the Atlantic coast from the Carolinas to New York. Rodgers was in command of the frigate *President* off the coast of [North Carolina](/wiki/North_Carolina "North Carolina"). On May 16, 1811, he sighted and followed the British sloop [*Little Belt*](/wiki/HMS_Little_Belt_(1807) "HMS Little Belt (1807)"), commanded by [Arthur Bingham](/wiki/Arthur_Bingham "Arthur Bingham"), thinking it to be HMS *Guerriere*. [Very different versions](/wiki/Little_Belt_affair "Little Belt affair") of events were given on either side.[[27]](#cite_note-33) A gun was fired, with each side accusing the other of the first shot.[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChisholm1911-32) Rodgers continued to engage the much smaller vessel and the *President*, bearing 44 guns, cut to pieces *Little Belt*, with only 20 guns.[[28]](#cite_note-34) *Little Belt* lost 13 men killed, including a midshipman and a lieutenant, and 19 wounded, while *President* incurred only one wounded. The incident came to be known as the [*Little Belt* affair](/wiki/Little_Belt_affair "Little Belt affair"). It was one among many incidents between the United States and Britain that led to the War of 1812.[[29]](#cite_note-35)

### War of 1812

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Rodgers_(naval_officer,_born_1772)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: War of 1812")]

Main article: [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Combat_soutenu_le_23_Aout_1812_par_la_Fregate_le_President_des_Etats-Unis_d%27_Amerique%2C_et_la_Fregate_Anglais_le_Belvedere_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Combat_soutenu_le_23_Aout_1812_par_la_Fregate_le_President_des_Etats-Unis_d%27_Amerique%2C_et_la_Fregate_Anglais_le_Belvedere_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Combat_soutenu_le_23_Aout_1812_par_la_Fregate_le_President_des_Etats-Unis_d%27_Amerique,_et_la_Fregate_Anglais_le_Belvedere_(cropped).jpg)

USS *President* engaging HMS *Belvidera* on 23 August 1812

When the United States declared war against the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland") on June 18, 1812, many American ships lacked crews and were in need of repairs, while others were still away at sea.[[25]](#cite_note-Roosevelt_p.72-31) The only ships available for service at this time were berthed at New York, under the command of Commodore John Rodgers. These were Rodgers' own flagship, *President*, along with [*United States*](/wiki/USS_United_States_(1797) "USS United States (1797)"), commanded by Commodore [Stephen Decatur](/wiki/Stephen_Decatur "Stephen Decatur"), [*Congress*](/wiki/USS_Congress_(1799) "USS Congress (1799)"), commanded by Captain Smith,[[Note 7]](#cite_note-36) [*Hornet*](/wiki/USS_Hornet_(1805) "USS Hornet (1805)"), commanded by Captain Lawrence, and *Argus*, commanded by Lieutenant [Sinclair](/wiki/Arthur_Sinclair "Arthur Sinclair"). However, British warships in American waters at this time were relatively few in number and not themselves very representative of the overall might of the Royal Navy.[[25]](#cite_note-Roosevelt_p.72-31)

Fearing that Congress might consider confining all American ships to port, as soon as Rodgers received news of the June declaration of war, he departed New York Harbor with his squadron within the hour. In anticipation of the war, Rodgers had already had his squadron fitted and ready to embark on the high seas. Their first objective was a British fleet reported to have recently departed from the West Indies, and Rodgers set a course south-east in search of these ships.[[30]](#cite_note-37)[[31]](#cite_note-38) *President* passed [Sandy Hook](/wiki/Sandy_Hook,_New_Jersey "Sandy Hook, New Jersey") on June 21. In the early morning of June 23, a ship was spotted on the horizon to the north-east, which turned out to be the frigate [HMS *Belvidera*](/wiki/HMS_Belvidera_(1809) "HMS Belvidera (1809)"), commanded by Captain [Richard Byron](/wiki/Richard_Byron_(Royal_Navy_officer) "Richard Byron (Royal Navy officer)"). Rodgers immediately gave chase, with *Congress* following close behind. *Belvidera* had already been informed of the inevitability of war by a passing New York [pilot boat](/wiki/Pilot_boat "Pilot boat") and immediately turned about, crowded on all sails and began flight to the north-east with a fresh wind behind all ships coming from the west.[[32]](#cite_note-Roosevelt70-72-39)[[33]](#cite_note-cooper245-246-40)[[Note 8]](#cite_note-42)

USS *President* was an unusually fast frigate and by noon had gained on *Belvidera*, now some two and a half miles distant, approximately 75 miles south-west of [Nantucket](/wiki/Nantucket "Nantucket") island. While *President* was closing with *Belvidera*, Captain Byron began clearing the decks and made ready his stern guns. By 4:30 the wind had relaxed some but *Belvidera* was now close enough to be engaged. Seizing this first possibility, *President*'s forecastle [bow chasers](/wiki/Chase_gun "Chase gun") fired the first shot of the war, by Rodgers himself, with two more almost immediately following.[[34]](#cite_note-Roosevelt_p.74-41)[[35]](#cite_note-Cooper,_1856_p.245-43)[[36]](#cite_note-44) All three shots struck *Belvidera* at her stern, striking the rudder assembly and captain's quarters, killing or wounding nine men.[[Note 9]](#cite_note-45) With only a few more shots needed to disable the British vessel, *President* fired again. But the tide of battle turned when one of its guns burst, killing 16 men, and wounding others, including Rodgers, whose leg was broken.[[Note 10]](#cite_note-47) There was a pause of panic about the entire ship, as now every gun was suspected. Byron fired his stern chasers, killing another six men. *Belvidera* continued to fire, damaging the rigging and foresails. *President* continued chase, but without adequate foresails, began yawing and losing ground. *Belvidera* escaped and returned to [Halifax, Nova Scotia](/wiki/Halifax,_Nova_Scotia "Halifax, Nova Scotia"), carrying the news of the declaration of war.[[32]](#cite_note-Roosevelt70-72-39)[[33]](#cite_note-cooper245-246-40)

Rodgers' squadron patrolled the waters off the American upper East Coast until the end of August, 1812. He commanded *President* for most of the war, capturing 23 [prizes](/wiki/Prize_law "Prize law"), one of the most successful records in the conflict. On land, Rodgers rendered valuable service by transferring his command briefly to Baltimore in September 1814. During the [Battle of Baltimore](/wiki/Battle_of_Baltimore "Battle of Baltimore"), he defended the city's east side extensive dug-in fortifications, devised by the [Maryland Militia's](/wiki/Maryland_National_Guard "Maryland National Guard") state commander, Major General [Samuel Smith](/wiki/Samuel_Smith_(Maryland_politician) "Samuel Smith (Maryland politician)") on Loudenschlager's Hill (today's Hampstead Hill in western [Patterson Park](/wiki/Patterson_Park "Patterson Park")). This was known as "Rodgers' Bastion", one of several that held some of the nearly 100 pieces of [artillery](/wiki/Artillery "Artillery") with 20,000 American troops that Smith had amassed to face the British. He had already sent down the southeastern "Patapsco Neck" peninsula, Brigadier General [John Stricker's](/wiki/John_Stricker "John Stricker") regiments of the City Brigade at the [Battle of North Point](/wiki/Battle_of_North_Point "Battle of North Point"). Rodgers helped defend against the simultaneous British naval attack on [Fort McHenry](/wiki/Fort_McHenry "Fort McHenry") protecting the harbor, and when Washington was invaded and [occupied](/wiki/Burning_of_Washington "Burning of Washington"), the month before after the [Battle of Bladensburg](/wiki/Battle_of_Bladensburg "Battle of Bladensburg").[[38]](#cite_note-48)

Rodgers' home town of Havre de Grace was [raided](/wiki/Raid_on_Havre_de_Grace "Raid on Havre de Grace") by British forces led by [George Cockburn](/wiki/Sir_George_Cockburn,_10th_Baronet "Sir George Cockburn, 10th Baronet") in 1813. Cockburn's men sacked and burned Rodgers's home,[[39]](#cite_note-Hickey153-49)[[Note 11]](#cite_note-50) while Rodgers's mother, wife, and two sisters fled to a friend's house near Havre de Grace. British forces eventually reached this house, too and were under orders to burn it and others in the area. Rodgers' sister, Mrs. Goldsborough, pleaded with the officer in charge of the detail, begging him to prevent the burning of their house for the sake of their aging mother. The officer maintained that he was under strict orders and would have to obtain the consent of his commanding officer, whereupon Mrs. Goldsborough returned with the officer to again plead her case. The commanding officer agreed to spare the house, but by the time they had returned, it had already been set ablaze. However, the fire had not yet taken hold and upon hearing the news that the house was to be spared, the British saved the house from complete ruin.[[40]](#cite_note-51)

In April 1814, Rodgers returned to Havre de Grace, where he received orders to take command of [*Guerriere*](/wiki/USS_Guerriere_(1814) "USS Guerriere (1814)") at [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia"), bearing 53 guns. Early in May of that year, he had replaced Commodore [Alexander Murray](/wiki/Alexander_Murray_(1755%E2%80%931821) "Alexander Murray (1755–1821)"), as commander of the [Delaware](/wiki/Delaware "Delaware") squadron. Rodgers ordered [Lieutenant Charles Morgan](/wiki/Charles_W._Morgan_(naval_officer) "Charles W. Morgan (naval officer)") to take charge of the squadron to reorganize it, instructing him about outfitting the ships with armament and drilling the crew. On June 20, 1814, *Guerriere* was launched with a crew of 200 men, while more than 50,000 spectators gathered on the shores of the [Delaware River](/wiki/Delaware_River "Delaware River") and in small boats to witness this. During that summer Rodgers spent most of his time at [Philadelphia's naval yard](/wiki/Philadelphia_Navy_Yard "Philadelphia Navy Yard") outfitting this ship. The Delaware squadron comprised some 20 gunboats, sloops and [galleys](/wiki/Galley "Galley"), and was one among several fleets assigned to patrol the chief ports along the upper Atlantic coastline.[[41]](#cite_note-52)

### Burning of Washington

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Rodgers_(naval_officer,_born_1772)&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Burning of Washington")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Burning_of_Washington_1814.jpg/250px-Burning_of_Washington_1814.jpg)](/wiki/File:Burning_of_Washington_1814.jpg)

An illustration of the [burning of Washington](/wiki/Burning_of_Washington "Burning of Washington") in 1814

Main article: [Burning of Washington](/wiki/Burning_of_Washington "Burning of Washington")

Commodore John Rodgers played a major role in the reoccupation of Washington after it had been [captured by British](/wiki/Burning_of_Washington "Burning of Washington") in 1814. As a naval officer he was generally unfamiliar with the tactics and deployments of land battle, yet he restored order after the occupation of Washington and he coordinated orders from Secretary of the Navy [William Jones](/wiki/William_Jones_(statesman) "William Jones (statesman)") for the employment of marines and sailors as naval infantry. Along with ground forces under his two principal subordinates, Commodore Oliver Hazard Perry and Commodore [David Porter](/wiki/David_Porter_(naval_officer) "David Porter (naval officer)"), Rodgers' flotilla of ships on the [Potomac River](/wiki/Potomac_River "Potomac River") forced the British to retreat.[[42]](#cite_note-Paullin3-53)[[43]](#cite_note-Roosevelt2-54)

In the summer of 1814, American naval forces in the Chesapeake Bay consisted mainly of a fleet of gunboats under the command of Commodore [Joshua Barney](/wiki/Joshua_Barney "Joshua Barney"), a veteran of the American Revolution. On August 20, a British fleet commanded by Cockburn sailed up the [Patuxent River](/wiki/Patuxent_River "Patuxent River") searching for Barney's flotilla while British troops marched in the same direction along its shore. Secretary of the Navy Jones responded by ordering Commodore Rodgers in Philadelphia and Commodore Porter in New York to proceed towards Washington with several detachments of sailors and marines. The orders were dispatched by mail but did not reach Philadelphia until ten o'clock the morning of the 22nd. As Rodgers was at [Reedy Island](/wiki/Reedy_Island "Reedy Island") on the Delaware River inspecting his flotilla he did not receive the Secretary's orders until he returned at eleven o'clock that evening—-thirteen hours later. Upon receiving the dispatch Rodgers immediately made preparations to march towards Baltimore. Secretary Jones, not knowing that his initial orders had reached Rodgers later than he had anticipated, expected Rodgers to be at his designated station by the evening of the 23rd, and had sent him follow up orders that morning directing Rodgers to [Bladensburg, Maryland](/wiki/Bladensburg,_Maryland "Bladensburg, Maryland"), five miles (8.0 km) north-east of Washington. Consequently, Rodgers did not receive his orders until it was too late to execute them.[[42]](#cite_note-Paullin3-53)[[43]](#cite_note-Roosevelt2-54)

By August 24, Admiral Cockburn's forces had already moved up the Patuxent, forcing Barney to abandon and burn his flotilla. With the area secured, Cockburn's forces advanced on Washington. That afternoon they defeated American troops under [General William Winder](/wiki/William_H._Winder "William H. Winder") and Commodore Barney at Bladensburg; by 8 o'clock that evening British troops entered Washington. Within twenty four hours, under the direct supervision of Admiral Cockburn, the British force set fire to the [Capitol building](/wiki/United_States_Capitol "United States Capitol"), the [White House](/wiki/White_House "White House"), and other government structures.[[44]](#cite_note-55) With American forces defeated and in retreat, President James Madison and Secretary Jones had fled the capital and made their way up the Potomac River to remain in hiding in the countryside. Rodgers proceeded to Baltimore, arriving on the 25th. The citizens there were in a panic fearing their city would suffer the same fate as had just befallen Washington. In the panic the Americans burned *Columbia* and *Argus* which were nearby, ready for service.[[45]](#cite_note-Roosevelt_p.319-56) Upon Rodgers' arrival he immediately took up preparing defensive measures about the area, the actions of which restored order among the citizenry; with the inhabitants' courage somewhat restored, Rodgers combined his command with that of Porter's and secured a small flotilla on the [Patapsco River](/wiki/Patapsco_River "Patapsco River"), which flows south-east into Chesapeake Bay at Baltimore. With a force of some thousand sailors and marines Rodgers set up defenses about Baltimore, dividing this force into two regiments, one under the command of Porter, the other under the command of Oliver Hazard Perry, who already had been stationed in Baltimore.[[42]](#cite_note-Paullin3-53)[[43]](#cite_note-Roosevelt2-54)

In the meantime, President Madison and Secretary Jones returned to Washington, but by August 27 British naval forces under the command of [Captain James Gordon](/wiki/James_Gordon_(Royal_Navy_officer) "James Gordon (Royal Navy officer)") advanced on the capital a second time, making their approach by way of the Potomac River with two frigates and a number of smaller vessels reaching [Fort Washington](/wiki/Fort_Washington,_Maryland "Fort Washington, Maryland"), twelve miles down river from the capital. The fort was abandoned when fired upon; the American forces retreated to [Alexandria](/wiki/Alexandria,_Virginia "Alexandria, Virginia"), five miles up river, just seven miles (11 km) outside of Washington. On August 29, Gordon advanced on and captured this town and port, seizing supplies which were then loaded aboard the invading vessels. Upon receiving orders to join Admiral Cockburn's squadron to the south, Gordon's flotilla sailed down river but was delayed due to adverse winds near Fort Washington. Fearing the British had further designs on the capital, Secretary Jones again began preparing defensive forces.

On August 29, he sent Rodgers orders to proceed to Bladensburg from Baltimore with 650 seamen and marines. The day before, Rodgers had ordered Porter to Washington; Porter's 100 sailors and a handful of officers arrived on August 30 with the purpose of guarding the capital. The next day Rodgers and Porter together arrived at Bladensburg where Rodgers met with Secretary Jones. As the American forces were regrouped and in strong defensive positions, the British decided to withdraw. The American forces commanded by Porter and Perry began harassing the retreating British while Rodgers was attacking the British fleet with [fire ships](/wiki/Fire_ship "Fire ship"). Rodgers had previously improvised his fireships at the Washington Navy Yard. On September 3, he proceeded down the Potomac in a [Gig](/wiki/Captain%27s_gig "Captain's gig") closely followed by his fireships and [barges](/wiki/Barge "Barge"), the latter being manned with 60 marines armed with [muskets](/wiki/Musket "Musket") and swords. When they reached Alexandria, Rodgers entered the abandoned town and ordered the American flag hoisted.[[42]](#cite_note-Paullin3-53)[[43]](#cite_note-Roosevelt2-54) Other battles followed with the British attempting to mount counteroffensives on the Potomac and at Baltimore, but these were ultimately frustrated largely through the efforts of forces commanded by Rodgers and Porter.[[42]](#cite_note-Paullin3-53)[[43]](#cite_note-Roosevelt2-54)

### President of the Board of Navy Commissioners, 1815–1824

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Rodgers_(naval_officer,_born_1772)&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: President of the Board of Navy Commissioners, 1815–1824")]

In 1815, after the War of 1812 had ended, Congress established the [Board of Navy Commissioners](/wiki/Board_of_Navy_Commissioners "Board of Navy Commissioners") (BNC).[[46]](#cite_note-57) Rodgers was a prolific political writer whose thoughts appealed to President Madison, leading him, with the consent of the Senate, to appoint Rodgers to the Board of Navy Commissioners, along with [Isaac Hull](/wiki/Isaac_Hull "Isaac Hull") and David Porter. Rodgers headed the Board from 1815 through 1824 and again from 1827 until he retired in May 1837.[[47]](#cite_note-Waldo,_1821_p.396-58) Rodgers also served briefly as Secretary of the Navy in 1823.[[4]](#cite_note-Paullin,_1910_p.325-4)
In February 1815, Commodore John Rodgers was appointed the President of the Board of Navy Commissioners. The law creating the Board gave it authority over procurement of naval stores, supplies and material as these related to the construction of naval vessels, outfitting of ships, armament and equipment plus oversight authority over naval shipyards, stations and dry-docks. The BNC also regulated civilian employment and pay. Serving with Rodgers on the Board were Commodores David Porter and Isaac Hull. The BNC found all current yards to have shortcomings. One of the most important recommendations the BNC made during Rodgers tenure was that only Portsmouth and Boston Navy Yard were suitable for the building of large ships in all seasons. Though the BNC recommended Washington Navy Yard be retained its report noted, "The Yard at Washington, when viewed as building yard only, would be less objectionable [than Baltimore] were the navigation deeper and the obstructions fewer. But it is the decided opinion of the Board, that the obstructions and its distance from the sea render it unsuitable for every other purpose."[[48]](#cite_note-59) The Board recommended Baltimore, Norfolk and [Charleston](/wiki/Charleston,_South_Carolina "Charleston, South Carolina") navy yards be closed. These recommendations were controversial and became the subject of considerable partisanship, with only Baltimore and Charleston eventually phased out of existence.[[49]](#cite_note-60) The BNC final report was in fact, highly critical of the Washington Navy Yard and its business practices. Writing on 11 May 1815 to Commodore Thomas Tingey the BNC stated, "The Board are about contracting for the repairs of the Black Smith shop in the Navy Yard under your command for the purpose of employing workmen to put in order for service & a state of preservation ... It is the intention of the Board of the Navy Commissioners, to reestablish the Navy Yard at this place, as a building Yard only, & while stating to you this intention, it may not be improper for them to make you acquainted with their views generally with respect to the establishment. They have witnessed in many of our Navy Yards & this particularly pressure in the employment of characters unsuited for the public service – maimed & unmanageable slaves for the accommodation of distressed widows & orphans & indigent families - apprentices for the accommodation of their masters – & old men & children for the benefit of their families & parents . These practices must cease – none must be employed but for the advantage of the public, & this Yard instead of rendering the navy odious to the nation from the scenes of want & extravagance which it has too long exhibited must serve as a model on which to prefect a general system of economy. In making to you,- Sir, these remarks the Navy Commissioners are aware that you have with themselves long witnessed the evils of which they complain, & which every countenance will be given to assist you in remedying them, they calculate with confidence on a disposition on your part to forward the public interests."[[50]](#cite_note-61) From November 1824 through May 1827, he commanded the [Mediterranean Squadron](/wiki/Mediterranean_Squadron_(United_States) "Mediterranean Squadron (United States)").[[51]](#cite_note-DeMeissner-62) After his final naval command, returned to New York where he became the Navy agent at the port there.

## Societies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Rodgers_(naval_officer,_born_1772)&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Societies")]

During the 1820s, Rodgers was a member of the prestigious [Columbian Institute for the Promotion of Arts and Sciences](/wiki/Columbian_Institute_for_the_Promotion_of_Arts_and_Sciences "Columbian Institute for the Promotion of Arts and Sciences"), who counted among their members former presidents [Andrew Jackson](/wiki/Andrew_Jackson "Andrew Jackson") and [John Quincy Adams](/wiki/John_Quincy_Adams "John Quincy Adams") and many prominent men of the day, including well-known representatives of the military, government service, medical and other professions.[[52]](#cite_note-63)

## Final years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Rodgers_(naval_officer,_born_1772)&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Final years")]

Several years before Rodgers retired from the Board of Naval Commissioners his health began to decline, it is believed from a case of [cholera](/wiki/Cholera "Cholera"). On advice that his condition would benefit from a leave of absence he was persuaded to take a trip across the Atlantic to England. Rodgers subsequently resigned his commission with the blessing of President [Andrew Jackson](/wiki/Andrew_Jackson "Andrew Jackson") and Secretary [Mahlon Dickerson](/wiki/Mahlon_Dickerson "Mahlon Dickerson") of the Navy. On May 10 he sailed for [London](/wiki/London "London"), embarking from New York on the [packet ship](/wiki/Packet_ship "Packet ship") *Montreal* and spent several weeks in London. He also visited the towns of [Plymouth](/wiki/Plymouth "Plymouth") and [Portsmouth](/wiki/Portsmouth "Portsmouth") and was escorted and given much attention by the [Admiralty](/wiki/Admiralty_(United_Kingdom) "Admiralty (United Kingdom)") and many notable people. He was the guest of two close friends, [Admiral Sir James Stirling](/wiki/James_Stirling_(Royal_Navy_officer) "James Stirling (Royal Navy officer)") and Lady Hillyarm who were with the [Mediterranean Fleet](/wiki/Mediterranean_Fleet "Mediterranean Fleet") while Rodgers was serving there, dealing with the piracy of the Barbary states.[[53]](#cite_note-final_years-64)

Late in August 1837 Rodgers returned to the United States with little improvement in his health. He remained at his home at [Lafayette Square](/wiki/Lafayette_Square_Historic_District_(Washington,_D.C.) "Lafayette Square Historic District (Washington, D.C.)") in Washington for several weeks, but with his health now steadily declining again he was placed in the care of the naval asylum at Philadelphia under the care of a naval doctor and friend, Dr. Thomas Harris. His wife took up residence in a boarding house nearby. Soon his already frail condition began to rapidly worsen and when it was certain his death was imminent his wife was sent for, but Rodgers had already lapsed into unconsciousness by the time she arrived at his bedside. Rodgers' last words were spoken to his butler and close friend, asking, *"...do you know the Lord's Prayer?"* His butler replied *"yes, master."* Rodgers responded, *"Then repeat it for me"*. Rodgers died in the arms of his butler on August 1, 1838, at the age of 66.[[47]](#cite_note-Waldo,_1821_p.396-58)[[53]](#cite_note-final_years-64)

Rodgers' funeral took place at the home of Commodore Biddle. In attendance was Brigadier General Prevost, who had called upon the uniformed men in the city to honor Rodgers with a parade through Washington.[[54]](#cite_note-65)

Rodgers was buried in the family burial site in the Congressional Cemetery at Washington, his grave marked by a pyramidal shaped sandstone monument which also bears the names of his wife, Minerva Denison, his son Frederick, and two daughters who were also laid to rest there in later years.[[8]](#cite_note-Paullin398-9)

## Influence and legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=John_Rodgers_(naval_officer,_born_1772)&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Influence and legacy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/WhalerSteamBark_Mary%26Helen_NewBedford_700x446.jpg/250px-WhalerSteamBark_Mary%26Helen_NewBedford_700x446.jpg)](/wiki/File:WhalerSteamBark_Mary%26Helen_NewBedford_700x446.jpg)

[First USS *Rodgers*](/wiki/USS_Rodgers_(1879) "USS Rodgers (1879)")

Commodore Rodgers established a naval "dynasty" that produced several other notable officers. His son [John Rodgers](/wiki/John_Rodgers_(naval_officer,_Civil_War) "John Rodgers (naval officer, Civil War)") (1812–1882) served in the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"), and his great-grandson [John Rodgers](/wiki/John_Rodgers_(naval_officer,_World_War_I) "John Rodgers (naval officer, World War I)") (1881–1926) served in [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I").

Six ships have been named in their honor, three as [USS *John Rodgers*](/wiki/USS_John_Rodgers "USS John Rodgers") and three as [USS *Rodgers*](/wiki/USS_Rodgers "USS Rodgers").

Louisa, daughter of Commodore Rodgers, was married to Union General [Montgomery C. Meigs](/wiki/Montgomery_C._Meigs_(1816%E2%80%931892) "Montgomery C. Meigs (1816–1892)"); their son [John Rodgers Meigs](/wiki/John_Rodgers_Meigs "John Rodgers Meigs") was killed in the Civil War in 1864. (General Meigs was a great grandson of [Continental Army](/wiki/Continental_Army "Continental Army") Colonel [Return J. Meigs, Sr.](/wiki/Return_J._Meigs,_Sr. "Return J. Meigs, Sr.")).

His home, [Sion Hill](/wiki/Sion_Hill "Sion Hill"), was added to the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places") in 1990, and designated a [National Historic Landmark](/wiki/National_Historic_Landmark "National Historic Landmark") in 1992.
